note_data.title: Urbanisation
keyPoints: **Urbanisation** is the increase in the proportion of people living in urban areas compared to rural areas
**Push factors** drive people from rural areas including lack of employment, poor healthcare, natural disasters, and agricultural mechanisation
**Pull factors** attract people to urban areas including better job opportunities, improved services, education, and entertainment facilities
**Megacities** are urban areas with populations over 10 million people, such as Karachi which has over 16 million inhabitants
**Squatter settlements** are illegal housing areas built on land not owned by the inhabitants, often using temporary materials
**Shanty towns** develop due to rapid rural-urban migration when formal housing cannot meet demand, lacking basic services like water and electricity
Urban growth creates **housing shortages** leading to overcrowding and development of informal settlements on marginal land
**Traffic congestion** increases in cities due to more vehicles, inadequate road infrastructure, and poor public transport systems
Urban areas face **pollution problems** including air pollution from vehicles and industries, and water pollution from inadequate sewage treatment

definitions.definition: The increase in the proportion of people living in urban areas compared to rural areas
An urban area with a population of over 10 million people
Illegal housing built on land not owned by the inhabitants, using temporary materials and lacking basic services
The movement of people from countryside areas to towns and cities
An area of poor quality housing, lacking in amenities such as water supply, sewerage and electricity

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give detailed characteristics or features - what you can see
Give a logical reason or solution based on the evidence provided

modelAnswer.question: Explain why people migrate from rural areas to cities in developing countries. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: People migrate due to push factors from rural areas. Agricultural mechanisation reduces the need for farm workers, so unemployment increases. Natural disasters such as floods destroy crops and homes, forcing people to move. Rural areas often lack healthcare facilities and schools, making life difficult. Pull factors attract people to cities. Cities offer more employment opportunities in factories and services. Better healthcare and education facilities are available in urban areas. Cities provide entertainment and modern lifestyle opportunities that rural areas cannot offer.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Push factor - natural disasters/poor services

commonMistakes: Never say 'people want better lives' - always specify what attracts them (employment, services, education)
Students often forget to distinguish between push and pull factors - always identify which type
Don't just list problems of cities - explain why these problems occur due to rapid urbanisation
Never say 'squatters are lazy' - explain they have no choice due to housing shortages and poverty
Students confuse shanty towns with slums - shanty towns are temporary, slums can be permanent but poor quality
examinerTips: This topic appears almost every year in Paper 2 - often linked with population questions
Always use specific examples like Karachi, Lahore or Orangi Town for Pakistani context
Case study of a city in a developing country is frequently examined - learn Karachi well
Problems and solutions questions are common - learn both negative and positive impacts
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Urban land use model showing CBD in center, surrounded by inner city (including squatter settlements), suburbs, and rural-urban fringe. Include transport routes and direction of urban growth.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to compare counterurbanisation with re-urbanisation processes
This topic was tested in O/N 2022 P1 Q5 (4 marks) — they asked students to explain consequences of re-urbanisation with examples
This topic was tested in M/J 2024 P2 Q2 (8 marks) — they asked students to evaluate whether urbanisation is the main factor in urban environmental degradation
This topic was tested in O/N 2023 P1 Q3 (4 marks) — they asked students to explain consequences of counterurbanisation for urban areas

workedExample.question: With aid of examples, explain the consequences of re-urbanisation. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define re-urbanisation as the movement of people back into urban areas that have previously experienced decline
Step 2: Identify key consequences: gentrification, displacement, improved services, increased property prices
Step 3: Use specific examples like London Docklands, Detroit downtown revival, or Barcelona's regeneration
Step 4: Link consequences to examples with clear explanation of cause and effect
workedExample.answer: Re-urbanisation leads to gentrification where wealthier residents move into previously declining urban areas, as seen in London's Docklands where former industrial areas became luxury housing. This causes displacement of original low-income residents who can no longer afford rising property prices. However, re-urbanisation also brings improved services and infrastructure, such as better transport links and retail facilities in Detroit's downtown area following urban renewal projects. The process creates social segregation as different income groups become spatially separated within the urban area.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct definition/understanding, 1 mark for each consequence clearly explained with example (max 3 consequences), 1 mark for specific named example with detail

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused urbanisation with re-urbanisation' — always clearly distinguish between initial urban growth and the return of people to declining urban areas
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students failed to provide specific examples when asked' — always name actual places like London Docklands, Detroit, or Manchester when discussing urban regeneration
Examiner Report 2024: 'Candidates often described consequences without explaining the process' — always explain HOW re-urbanisation leads to gentrification and displacement, not just that it happens
note_data.keyDiagram: Urban Settlement Cycle Diagram: Draw a circular flow diagram showing the four stages - urbanisation (rural to urban migration), suburbanisation (movement to city edges), counterurbanisation (movement to rural areas), and re-urbanisation (return to city centers). Include arrows showing population movement directions, timeframes (urbanisation: industrial period, suburbanisation: 1950s-70s, counterurbanisation: 1970s-90s, re-urbanisation: 1990s-present), and driving factors for each stage (job opportunities, car ownership, quality of life, urban regeneration).
note_data.quickRecap: Urbanisation is the increase in proportion of people living in urban areas, driven by rural-urban migration and natural increase. It follows a cycle: initial urbanisation, then suburbanisation (movement to edges), counterurbanisation (movement to rural areas), and re-urbanisation (return to city centers). Key consequences include gentrification, displacement of original residents, improved services, and increased property prices. Always use specific examples like London Docklands for re-urbanisation.
